3: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Meat demand has increased significantly in recent decades and it has grown faster than the world population, due to the change in diets and the increase of living standards, allowing more people to consume meat in larger quantities (Keyzer et al., 2005). Pig meat

consumption increase periodically, reaching 112.4 million tons in 2012, and becoming the most widely consumed worldwide (FAO, 2014). In 2016, this trade increased by 4.4% compared to the pre- vious year, being the world's highest meat production, compared to beef, lamb and poultry (FAO, 2016; OECD, 2017). It is predicted that pig meat production in 2050 will be 465 million tons, doubling the production reported in 2000 (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Besides, according to FAO, the agricultural sector is responsible for 18% of global greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions. Livestock intensive production negatively impacts the environment (Basset-
Nomenclature

AD|Anaerobic digestion BOD|Biochemical oxygen demand, kg BOD5 CC|Climate change, kg CO2-eq COD|Chemical oxygen demand, kg COD FE|Freshwater eutrophication, kg P-eq FDD|Fixed dome digester FU|Functional unit, kg GHG|Greenhouse gases LCA|Life Cycle Assessment ME|Marine eutrophication, kg N-eq PELU|Porcine equivalent livestock unit PPT|Pig production technologies TA|Terrestrial acidification, kg SO2-eq TET|Terrestrial ecotoxicity, kg 1,4-Dichlorobenzene-eq UASB|Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket

Mens and Van der Werf, 2005; Pirlo et al., 2016; Sage, 2011), generating around 668 million tons of CO2-equivalent (eq) year-1 from pig meat production (MacLeod et al., 2013); 606 million tons CO2-eq year from chicken production (meat, eggs and protein); and 2836.8 million tons CO2-eq year from beef meat production (Opio et al., 2013), affecting ecosystems and contributing negatively to climate change, and human and environmental health (Pirlo et al., 2016).

In Cuba, the conceptualization of the economic and social development model (PCC, 2017) makes emphasis on efficient food production, particularly pig meat production. According to ONEI (2017), pig meat production represents 60% of the total meat pro- duced in Cuba, mainly in the state-owned sector (69.5%). Pig pro- duction in Sancti Spíritus province has reached values from 6.5 to 8% of the national production with an average of 118 thousand pigs in the last five years, where 70-80% of these pigs heads are concentrated in state-owned farms (ONEI, 2017). State-owned pig farms in Cuba are divided into three pig production technologies (PPT): Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms. In 2015, these technologies contributed to 72% of the total pig pro- duction in Sancti Spiritus province (ONEI, 2016). However, the characteristics of each PPT differ according to their function, influencing the animal feed demand, the energy requirements, the amount of wastes generated and the waste treatment technologies; affecting in different ways their environmental profiles.

Although Cuban laws regulate waste management from pig production to control the discharge of potential polluting wastes (Gaceta Oficial, 1997, 2017), negative environmental impacts persist due to the installation of low efficiency technologies (Pérez et al., 2016). Lagooning is the main technology for the treatment of pig waste in Cuba (Gutiérrez et al., 2016), where emissions of methane (CH4), ammonia (NH3), carbon dioxide (CO2) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) occur from the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter (Safley Jr and Westerman, 1988; Stone et al., 2010; Toprak, 1995). The liquid effluents (with chemical oxygen demand (COD) concentrations above 8000 mg L-¹ resulting from lagooning are typically used for fertirrigation of nearby plantations, while the sludge has been frequently used for fertilization (Barrera et al., 2016; Schmidt, 2013). Even though the deterioration of the tech- nical conditions in lagoons is affecting the overall waste treatment efficiency (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006) (usually found as a result of poor maintenance and operation, e.g., poor sludge drainage and overload of organic matter), the associated environmental impacts were not found in literature.

In the last years, small scale anaerobic digestion (AD) technol- logies has increased in applications as an alternative for cooking in rural communities, reporting a total amount of 3243 digesters (Ministerio de Comercio Exterior y la Inversión Extranjera, 2017). Fixed dome digesters (FDD) are among the most implemented technologies (~60% of the total), which possess a very low COD removal efficiency (maximum 50% (Nzila et al., 2012)). Although, several medium scale technologies (covered lagoon and hybrid technologies) have been installed in order to use pig wastes for the production of electricity and thermal energy, the use of high effi- ciency AD technologies (COD removal efficiencies from 80 to 90%) such as Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) for pig waste treatment has never been implemented in Cuba, despite its demonstrated feasibility (Kim et al., 2004; Najafpour et al., 2006). In this context, high efficiency technologies for waste treatment during pig production activities could bring environmental benefits for the Cuban context.

The Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology has been widely used to evaluate the environmental contribution of livestock pro- duction (Reckmann et al., 2012; Thomassen and De Boer, 2005; Van der Werf et al., 2005). LCA allows the assessment of process im- provements alternatives (e.g. relevant scenarios and novel tech- nologies) throughout its life cycle (Nguyen et al., 2011). Several authors have evaluated the environmental impacts associated to pig production, mainly in Europe and United States (Cherubini et al., 2015; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Lamnatou et al., 2016; Mackenzie et al., 2016; McAuliffe et al., 2017; Ogino et al., 2013; Van Zanten et al., 2015).

Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the environmental impact of intensive pig production in Cienfuegos, Cuba, considering the environmental impacts only for a PPT (unknown technology) and taking into account the contribution of pig production stages and activities. Despite AD was considered as a process improvement alternative, the impact of high efficiency technologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) and lagooning was not assessed. In addition, Peña, M.D. et al. (2013) studied pig production in Pal- mira, Cienfuegos, determining the Life Cycle Impact of the pro- ductive systems and considering AD as a process improvement alternative. The impacts of the activities during the productive process (e.g., animal feed and energy consumption), as well as the implementation of high efficiency AD technologies were not re- ported. In this context, a research necessity arises for the Envi- ronmental Life Cycle Assessment of Cuban pig production considering the activities involved in Genetic, Multiplier and Pro- duction Farms; as well as the specific conditions and characteristic of the waste treatment technologies for process improvement.

Therefore, the objective of this study was to evaluate the Life Cycle Environmental Impacts of three Pig Production Technologies (Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms), consid- ering the activities involved in their productive systems (pig pro- duction, animal feed, avoided fertilizer production, energy and water consumption) and relevant cleaner production scenarios for the Cuban context; such as: 1) the impact of the technical condi- tions during lagooning; and 2) the use of high efficiency AD tech- nologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) for waste management. The province of Sancti Spíritus was taken as case study considering the existence of similar scenarios in all Cuban provinces.

Mens and Van der Werf, 2005; Pirlo et al., 2016; Sage, 2011), generating around 668 million tons of CO2-equivalent (eq) year-1 from pig meat production (MacLeod et al., 2013); 606 million tons CO2-eq year from chicken production (meat, eggs and protein); and 2836.8 million tons CO2-eq year from beef meat production (Opio et al., 2013), affecting ecosystems and contributing negatively to climate change, and human and environmental health (Pirlo et al., 2016).

In Cuba, the conceptualization of the economic and social development model (PCC, 2017) makes emphasis on efficient food production, particularly pig meat production. According to ONEI (2017), pig meat production represents 60% of the total meat pro- duced in Cuba, mainly in the state-owned sector (69.5%). Pig pro- duction in Sancti Spíritus province has reached values from 6.5 to 8% of the national production with an average of 118 thousand pigs in the last five years, where 70-80% of these pigs heads are concentrated in state-owned farms (ONEI, 2017). State-owned pig farms in Cuba are divided into three pig production technologies (PPT): Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms. In 2015, these technologies contributed to 72% of the total pig pro- duction in Sancti Spiritus province (ONEI, 2016). However, the characteristics of each PPT differ according to their function, influencing the animal feed demand, the energy requirements, the amount of wastes generated and the waste treatment technologies; affecting in different ways their environmental profiles.

Although Cuban laws regulate waste management from pig production to control the discharge of potential polluting wastes (Gaceta Oficial, 1997, 2017), negative environmental impacts persist due to the installation of low efficiency technologies (Pérez et al., 2016). Lagooning is the main technology for the treatment of pig waste in Cuba (Gutiérrez et al., 2016), where emissions of methane (CH4), ammonia (NH3), carbon dioxide (CO2) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) occur from the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter (Safley Jr and Westerman, 1988; Stone et al., 2010; Toprak, 1995). The liquid effluents (with chemical oxygen demand (COD) concentrations above 8000 mg L-¹ resulting from lagooning are typically used for fertirrigation of nearby plantations, while the sludge has been frequently used for fertilization (Barrera et al., 2016; Schmidt, 2013). Even though the deterioration of the tech- nical conditions in lagoons is affecting the overall waste treatment efficiency (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006) (usually found as a result of poor maintenance and operation, e.g., poor sludge drainage and overload of organic matter), the associated environmental impacts were not found in literature.

In the last years, small scale anaerobic digestion (AD) technol- logies has increased in applications as an alternative for cooking in rural communities, reporting a total amount of 3243 digesters (Ministerio de Comercio Exterior y la Inversión Extranjera, 2017). Fixed dome digesters (FDD) are among the most implemented technologies (~60% of the total), which possess a very low COD removal efficiency (maximum 50% (Nzila et al., 2012)). Although, several medium scale technologies (covered lagoon and hybrid technologies) have been installed in order to use pig wastes for the production of electricity and thermal energy, the use of high effi- ciency AD technologies (COD removal efficiencies from 80 to 90%) such as Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) for pig waste treatment has never been implemented in Cuba, despite its demonstrated feasibility (Kim et al., 2004; Najafpour et al., 2006). In this context, high efficiency technologies for waste treatment during pig production activities could bring environmental benefits for the Cuban context.

The Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology has been widely used to evaluate the environmental contribution of livestock pro- duction (Reckmann et al., 2012; Thomassen and De Boer, 2005; Van der Werf et al., 2005). LCA allows the assessment of process im- provements alternatives (e.g. relevant scenarios and novel tech- nologies) throughout its life cycle (Nguyen et al., 2011). Several authors have evaluated the environmental impacts associated to pig production, mainly in Europe and United States (Cherubini et al., 2015; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Lamnatou et al., 2016; Mackenzie et al., 2016; McAuliffe et al., 2017; Ogino et al., 2013; Van Zanten et al., 2015).

Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the environmental impact of intensive pig production in Cienfuegos, Cuba, considering the environmental impacts only for a PPT (unknown technology) and taking into account the contribution of pig production stages and activities. Despite AD was considered as a process improvement alternative, the impact of high efficiency technologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) and lagooning was not assessed. In addition, Peña, M.D. et al. (2013) studied pig production in Pal- mira, Cienfuegos, determining the Life Cycle Impact of the pro- ductive systems and considering AD as a process improvement alternative. The impacts of the activities during the productive process (e.g., animal feed and energy consumption), as well as the implementation of high efficiency AD technologies were not re- ported. In this context, a research necessity arises for the Envi- ronmental Life Cycle Assessment of Cuban pig production considering the activities involved in Genetic, Multiplier and Pro- duction Farms; as well as the specific conditions and characteristic of the waste treatment technologies for process improvement.

Therefore, the objective of this study was to evaluate the Life Cycle Environmental Impacts of three Pig Production Technologies (Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms), consid- ering the activities involved in their productive systems (pig pro- duction, animal feed, avoided fertilizer production, energy and water consumption) and relevant cleaner production scenarios for the Cuban context; such as: 1) the impact of the technical condi- tions during lagooning; and 2) the use of high efficiency AD tech- nologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) for waste management. The province of Sancti Spíritus was taken as case study considering the existence of similar scenarios in all Cuban provinces.

In the last years, small scale anaerobic digestion (AD) technol- logies has increased in applications as an alternative for cooking in rural communities, reporting a total amount of 3243 digesters (Ministerio de Comercio Exterior y la Inversión Extranjera, 2017). Fixed dome digesters (FDD) are among the most implemented technologies (~60% of the total), which possess a very low COD removal efficiency (maximum 50% (Nzila et al., 2012)). Although, several medium scale technologies (covered lagoon and hybrid technologies) have been installed in order to use pig wastes for the production of electricity and thermal energy, the use of high effi- ciency AD technologies (COD removal efficiencies from 80 to 90%) such as Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) for pig waste treatment has never been implemented in Cuba, despite its demonstrated feasibility (Kim et al., 2004; Najafpour et al., 2006). In this context, high efficiency technologies for waste treatment during pig production activities could bring environmental benefits for the Cuban context.

The Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology has been widely used to evaluate the environmental contribution of livestock pro- duction (Reckmann et al., 2012; Thomassen and De Boer, 2005; Van der Werf et al., 2005). LCA allows the assessment of process im- provements alternatives (e.g. relevant scenarios and novel tech- nologies) throughout its life cycle (Nguyen et al., 2011). Several authors have evaluated the environmental impacts associated to pig production, mainly in Europe and United States (Cherubini et al., 2015; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Lamnatou et al., 2016; Mackenzie et al., 2016; McAuliffe et al., 2017; Ogino et al., 2013; Van Zanten et al., 2015).

Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the environmental impact of intensive pig production in Cienfuegos, Cuba, considering the environmental impacts only for a PPT (unknown technology) and taking into account the contribution of pig production stages and activities. Despite AD was considered as a process improvement alternative, the impact of high efficiency technologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) and lagooning was not assessed. In addition, Peña, M.D. et al. (2013) studied pig production in Pal- mira, Cienfuegos, determining the Life Cycle Impact of the pro- ductive systems and considering AD as a process improvement alternative. The impacts of the activities during the productive process (e.g., animal feed and energy consumption), as well as the implementation of high efficiency AD technologies were not re- ported. In this context, a research necessity arises for the Envi- ronmental Life Cycle Assessment of Cuban pig production considering the activities involved in Genetic, Multiplier and Pro- duction Farms; as well as the specific conditions and characteristic of the waste treatment technologies for process improvement.

Therefore, the objective of this study was to evaluate the Life Cycle Environmental Impacts of three Pig Production Technologies (Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms), consid- ering the activities involved in their productive systems (pig pro- duction, animal feed, avoided fertilizer production, energy and water consumption) and relevant cleaner production scenarios for the Cuban context; such as: 1) the impact of the technical condi- tions during lagooning; and 2) the use of high efficiency AD tech- nologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digester (i.e. FDD) for waste management. The province of Sancti Spíritus was taken as case study considering the existence of similar scenarios in all Cuban provinces.
11: The LCA study was carried out to assess the environmental impact of the Cuban pig production farms (Case Study: Sancti Spíritus), considering cleaner production scenarios.
12: The goal of this study was to assess the environmental impact associated with three PPT (Genetic, Multiplier and Production) in Sancti Spíritus province (Cuba), through a Life Cycle Assessment methodology considering cleaner production scenarios. The tech- nology with the best environmental profile was selected, and cleaner production scenarios were assessed to reach a better environmental performance. The functional unit, system bound- aries, characteristic of the pig production in Sancti Spiritus, main assumptions, Life cycle inventory analysis, Life cycle impact assessment and interpretation are described in details below.
13: The function of a pig farm is to produce alive pigs in different stages. For that reason, a porcine equivalent livestock unit (PELU) of 50 kg, obtained from the parturition of 100 reproducers in a year, was considered as the functional unit in this study. It means that 100 reproducers will provide the amount of pigs depending of the number of births, breeding and deaths in each of the three PPT (Genetic, Multiplier and Production).

The stages of each PPT to obtain the PELU of 50 kg are presented in Fig. 1. Emissions to air, water and soil were considered within the system boundaries. The first PPT, Genetic farm (A-1) is responsible for obtaining pure race males and females for self-replacement in A-1 and producers replacement in Multiplier farms (A-2) and Production farms (A-3). This was composed of the different pig growth stages, including pig breeding, piglets, porkers and pre- fattening. It was considered that the liquid and solid wastes are always degraded in lagoons. Gaseous emissions from the enteric emissions of pigs were also considered. The second PPT, A-2, considered the females destined for reproducers self-replacement and included the stages of A-1 in addition to fattening of piglets.

The main purpose of the third PPT, A-3, is producing animals for slaughtering with high amount of lean meat and high economic efficiency, considering the stages of A-2, the fattening of porkers and the commercial fattening (Fig. 1).

The gaseous emissions of the enteric fermentation, as well as those of the lagoon are considered as emissions to the air (ecosphere); the components identified in the digestate of the lagoon are considered as emissions to the soil; and both the COD and Biochemical oxygen demand (BOD5) are considered as emis- sions to water. Soil occupation and water consumption are considered as resources taken from nature (ecosphere). The con- sumption of corn and soybeans are considered as inputs from the technosphere (materials). Electricity is considered as an entrance from the technosphere. The contents of nitrogen (N), phosphorous (P) and potassium (K) in lagoon digestate were considered as known outputs to the technosphere (avoided product).

The activities considered in this study are represented in Fig. 1. These include feed production, pig production (split into pig housing and lagooning), water consumption, electricity, feed transportation and avoided fertilizer production. Feed production included the main components of pig diet (corn and soybeans), pig production included pig housing with the growing stages starting from reproducers and going through all stages involved (depending on the PPT). Lagooning was always considered as the waste man- agement technology. Water consumption, electricity and feed transportation contemplated the use of water for cleaning, elec- tricity for illumination and pumping in indoor farms, and diesel consumption in fodder transportation outdoor, respectively. Avoi- ded fertilizer production is based on product avoided previously mentioned.

17: <header>2.3. Characteristics of the pig production in Sancti Spiritus </header>
Most of the pigs in Sancti Spiritus, as for the rest of Cuba, are

A-1: Considering only "Pre-fattening" of "Pig breeding (Genetic farm) A-2: Considering "Pre-fattening" of "Pig breedings and "Fattening] from "Piglets (Multiplier farm) A-3: Considering "Pre-fattening" of "Pig breedings", "Fattening] from "Piglets and Porkers (Reproduction farm)
Commercial fattening is only considered for A-3

concentrated in big farms, following the three PPT (A-1, A-2 and A- 3). As explained before, a pig production farm has several stages to cover the production cycle (Peña, J. et al., 2013). The average weight, duration through different growing stages, death rate and rejection index are shown in Table 1.
Pig breeding: Covers from pig birth until weaning, with a period of 35 days. The output weight was considered as 8 kg for all the cases.

Pre-fattening: This includes the rejected descendants, males or females, which are not necessary for self-replacement of special- ized herd, being intended for meat production. It considered ani- mals coming from weaning, which are rejected (do not meet the established requirements) during the genetic selection for repro- duction (A-1). In A-1, pre-fattening duration is from 36 to 45 days, while in A-2 and A-3 the duration is from 36 to 96 days. The average weight is 10 kg for A-1 and 40 kg for A-2 and A-3.
Piglets: Covers the animals that, at the weaning time, meet the established requirements for reproduction (in all PPT), from weaning to 110 days, reaching a minimum weight of 50 kg.
Porkers: This stage covers from the end of piglets categories (110 days), until the slaughtering at day 175. At the end of this stage, the average weight of pigs is at least 100 kg.
Fattening: This stage was considered in A-2 and A-3. Four types of fattening were considered.

<list>- Fattening-pre-fattening: Covers the animals from pre-fattening, from 97 to 240 days. 
- Fattening-piglets: Covers the animals rejected from piglets (do not meet the established requirements for reproduction), from 111 to 240 days. 
- Fattening-porkers: Covers the rejected porkers (do not meet the established weight requirements for reproduction), from 176 to 240 days. 
- Commercial fattening: Porkers enter to this stage in A-3 and covers from 176 to 240 days, reaching a maximum weight of 120 kg.</list>
Reproducers: All the alternatives included breeders and boars selected for reproduction with positive index (which meet the re- quirements to obtain good quality and fast growing pigs).

20: Genetic farm (A-1): This farm considered 100 reproducers with an index of 10 reproducers per stallion. Values of 2.2 births per reproducers and 10 breeding per birth in one year were considered. According to the process stage, farms have a specific rejection rate

(pigs that do not meet the parameters of growth and weight gain). This way, the rejection indexes for the different stages were: 1% for the pig breeding stage; 22.2% for piglets; and 1% for porkers. In addition, death rates indexes should be considered for the different stages, being: 12% for breeding, 5% for pre-fattening, 3% for piglets and 3% for pigs (Peña, J. et al., 2013) (Table 1).

Multiplier farm (A-2): An index of 13 reproducers per boar, with 2.2 births per reproducers and 10.2 breeding per birth in a year were considered. Similar to A-1, it was taken into account for pig breeding stage, the rejection index of 20% (since not all males were considered for reproduction), being around 15% for piglets and porkers. In that way, death rates were 12% for pig breeding, 3.3% for pre-fattening, 2% for piglets, 1% for porkers and 3% for all fattening (Peña, J. et al., 2013) (Table 1).

Production farm (A-3): It was considered an index of 10 re- producers per boar, with 2.7 births per reproducer and 10.1 breeding per birth in a year. It was taken into account a rejection index of around 15% for pig breeding stage and 1% for piglets and porkers. Besides, death rates were 11% for breeding stage, 4% for pre-fattening, 5% for piglets, 4% for porkers, and 5% for all fattening (Peña, J. et al., 2013) (Table 1).

22: Pig production is highly dependent of concentrated feed, espe- cially corn and soybeans (Cherubini et al., 2015; Ogino et al., 2013; Stone et al., 2012), reaching more than 80% of pig diets (Brazilian- Association-of-Swine-Breeders, 2016). This feed brings the main compounds for an adequate weight gain in the shortest time, ensuring efficiency in each stages of growth and developmental cycle (National Research Council, 1998). The feed for the pig pro- duction in Cuba is basically imported (Martín-Casas et al., 2017), being corn and soybeans the main constituents, which are im- ported from Brazil and United States respectively (Gutiérrez et al., 2016). The different feed formulations for the pigs growing stages in Cuba are shown in Table 2.
23: The main assumptions in this study are as follow:

The birth from 100 reproducers in one-year cycle was consid- ered taking into account an inventory in accordance with real conditions and the mathematical models used (integer values of pig in each stage). For example, if it is considered one reproducer as an input value, 0.01 death reproducers/year could be obtained.

<list>The consumption of energy for pumping, feed transportation and lighting, as well as the manure generated and fodder re- quirements were determined according to historical data of a pig production farm in Sancti Spiritus (Guayos Pig Enterprise). 
The amount of CH4 produced was calculated from the organic matter in pig waste, considering the generation of 0.35 m³ of biogas/kg volatile solid (VS) in manure. Biogas composition was considered as 60% of CH4 (Jiménez et al., 2015), and 40% of CO2 (Salomon and Lora, 2009). 
Manure was calculated as 5% of the pig weight, containing 20% of VS (Zarkadas and Pilidis, 2011). 
Incineration is widely used as a biosecurity method for pig disposal corpses (Morrow and Ferket, 2001; Sander et al., 2002). It was considered that the incineration of dead pigs generated emissions of CO2 and nitrous dioxide (NO), which were calcu- lated from the elemental composition of carbon (C) and N of pig components. These were assumed with a composition ratio 80:20% corresponding to meat:bones; where meat contains 41% of water, 11% of proteins, 47% of lipids and 1% of ashes; whereas bones contains 25% of water, 30% of proteins and 45% of ashes (Heinz and Hautzinger, 2007). 
The removal efficiency of the pig waste treatment lagoons was assumed as 50%, considering the average value of the evalua- tions reported in Toprak (1995). 
It was considered that 85% of the total water consumed at the Cuban piggeries leaves the process as liquid residue, because the remaining 15% is consumed by the animals or lost during the pig activities (Personal communication from Guayos Pig Enterprise). 
For the energy consumption, the Cuban electricity breakdown was assumed considering four different electricity sources ac- cording to ONEI (2010): centralized and decentralized thermal power plants (81.66%), combined cycles with gas turbine using liquefied petroleum gas (13.04%), cogeneration systems using bagasse (4.63%) and other technologies using renewable sources (0.67%). 
The use of products inside the facility in cleaning activities, antibiotics, disinfectants or antimicrobials were neglected as their contribution to the environmental impacts are not signif- icant (Luo et al., 2014; Stone et al., 2010). 
The COD concentration in the lagoon effluent was obtained from the model proposed by Toprak (1995) and Wu and Chen (2011). BOD5/COD ratio was considered as 43.4% (Montalvo, 1995). 
Pig enteric emissions (CO2, CH4 and NH3) was based on the study of Stone et al. (2010). 
Pig supply chain was out of the system boundaries for this study (e.g. feed transportation from abroad).</list>

<list>The consumption of energy for pumping, feed transportation and lighting, as well as the manure generated and fodder re- quirements were determined according to historical data of a pig production farm in Sancti Spiritus (Guayos Pig Enterprise). 
The amount of CH4 produced was calculated from the organic matter in pig waste, considering the generation of 0.35 m³ of biogas/kg volatile solid (VS) in manure. Biogas composition was considered as 60% of CH4 (Jiménez et al., 2015), and 40% of CO2 (Salomon and Lora, 2009). 
Manure was calculated as 5% of the pig weight, containing 20% of VS (Zarkadas and Pilidis, 2011). 
Incineration is widely used as a biosecurity method for pig disposal corpses (Morrow and Ferket, 2001; Sander et al., 2002). It was considered that the incineration of dead pigs generated emissions of CO2 and nitrous dioxide (NO), which were calcu- lated from the elemental composition of carbon (C) and N of pig components. These were assumed with a composition ratio 80:20% corresponding to meat:bones; where meat contains 41% of water, 11% of proteins, 47% of lipids and 1% of ashes; whereas bones contains 25% of water, 30% of proteins and 45% of ashes (Heinz and Hautzinger, 2007). 
The removal efficiency of the pig waste treatment lagoons was assumed as 50%, considering the average value of the evalua- tions reported in Toprak (1995). 
It was considered that 85% of the total water consumed at the Cuban piggeries leaves the process as liquid residue, because the remaining 15% is consumed by the animals or lost during the pig activities (Personal communication from Guayos Pig Enterprise). 
For the energy consumption, the Cuban electricity breakdown was assumed considering four different electricity sources ac- cording to ONEI (2010): centralized and decentralized thermal power plants (81.66%), combined cycles with gas turbine using liquefied petroleum gas (13.04%), cogeneration systems using bagasse (4.63%) and other technologies using renewable sources (0.67%). 
The use of products inside the facility in cleaning activities, antibiotics, disinfectants or antimicrobials were neglected as their contribution to the environmental impacts are not signif- icant (Luo et al., 2014; Stone et al., 2010). 
The COD concentration in the lagoon effluent was obtained from the model proposed by Toprak (1995) and Wu and Chen (2011). BOD5/COD ratio was considered as 43.4% (Montalvo, 1995). 
Pig enteric emissions (CO2, CH4 and NH3) was based on the study of Stone et al. (2010). 
Pig supply chain was out of the system boundaries for this study (e.g. feed transportation from abroad).</list>

27: The relevance of an LCA is related with the quality of the in- ventory assessment (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The data sources for the inventory in this study are described below. 2.5.1. Data acquisition. The data for the inventory analysis was provided by representatives of the Provincial Pig Enterprise of

Sancti Spiritus, representative pig producers from farms of the three PPT (Genetic farm Cabaiguan; Multiplier Tamarindo and In- tegral "Cacahual), specialists in pig feeding, academic experts and literature studies on LCA of pig production. All this data was calculated for a 0kg pig as the PELU (Table 3). According to the physiologic needs for each stage of growth of the pigs, nutritional values to reach an optimum yield were considered. The water consumption corresponds with the conventional technology of solid floor.

The inventory includes the fertilizers used in the cultivation of the principal components of pig feed, energy and other resources. The distance between the feed factory and the farm was estimated as 300 km (two-way trip). The contents of N, P and K in lagoon sludge was considered to substitute chemical fertilizers (Warman and Termeer, 2005). The avoided products were ammonium phosphate, triple super phosphate (as diphosphorus pentoxide, P2O5) and potassium chloride (as potassium oxide, K20).

29: <header>2.6. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and interpretation </header>

The ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al., 2013) was considered as impact assessment methodology (Ali et al., 2017; Cherubini et al., 2015; González-García et al., 2015; Lamnatou et al., 2016; Noya et al., 2017; Stone et al., 2012). The impact categories were considered in hierarchist perspective using Ecoinvent v2.2 database with the assistance of OpenLCA v1.3.0. software (Green Delta, 2014). After the identification of the impact categories, as part of the LCA methodology and taking into account the main emissions within the referred system boundary (CH4, CO2, NO2, NH3, BOD5 and COD), five impact categories were identified as being most used in LCA studies for pig production (Noya et al., 2017; Stone et al., 2012): climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), freshwater eutrophication (FE) and terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET).
30: The characterized global impacts for the three PPT (A-1, A-2 and A-3) are presented in Table 4 for the functional unit (PELU of 50 kg).

The studied categories showed global impacts with positive values (harmful for the environment). For all PPT, the order of environmental impacts follows as next A-1<A-2<A-3, being A-1 the best PPT due to the lowest consumption of feed (soybean and corn) and energy, and the lowest global emissions of pollutants (Table 3). In A-2 and A-3, a higher amount of feed was needed (17 and 20%, respectively) (Table because of the higher number of production stages (Fig. and PELU with respect to A-1; and hence, an increase in the amounts of manure and polluting gases for A-2 and A-3 are expected (Reckmann et al., 2012). For that reason, A-2 and A-3 behave similar for the studied impact categories (CC, FE, ME and TET), being from 19 to 41% higher than in A-1; suggesting that Multiplier and Reproduction Farms (A-2 and A-3) should intensify the implementation of cleaner production opportunities. 3.2. Breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities

In general, the breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities for the best PPT (A-1) was determined, considering as activities: pig production (including lagooning for manure treat- ment), feed production, electricity, water consumption, feed

transportation and avoided fertilizer production (Fig. 2). Pig pro- duction activity was identified as the main contributor to the global impact, with contributions from 75 to 89% for CC, FE, ME and TA, mainly because of CH4 and NO2 emissions during lagooning. Then, the recovery of CH4 and NO2 during the waste treatment in the pig production activity, become a necessity to mitigate the negative environmental impacts. For the impact category TET, the main contribution was feed production (87%), being in contrast with previous LCA studies, like Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and Reckmann et al. (2012), who suggested that pig production is the main contributor. A very low contribution was observed for electricity (below 0.54%) as it was only 1.46 and 2.69 kWh for lighting and

Fig. 2. A breakdown of the impact categories: CC, FE, ME, TA and TET for A-1. CC: Climate Change as kg CO2-eq, FE: Freshwater eutrophication as kg P-eq, ME: Marine eutrophication as kg N-eq, TA: Terrestrial acidification as kg SO2-eq, TET: Terrestrial ecotoxicity as kg 1.4-Dichlorobenzene-eq.

pumping, respectively (Table 3). The contribution of the remaining activities was negligible. All impact categories were benefited from the use of residues (liquids and solids) as biofertilizer, avoiding the use of chemical fertilizers. A further discussion on the contribution of the activities to the impact categories is detailed below.

3.2. Breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities

In general, the breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities for the best PPT (A-1) was determined, considering as activities: pig production (including lagooning for manure treat- ment), feed production, electricity, water consumption, feed

transportation and avoided fertilizer production (Fig. 2). Pig pro- duction activity was identified as the main contributor to the global impact, with contributions from 75 to 89% for CC, FE, ME and TA, mainly because of CH4 and NO2 emissions during lagooning. Then, the recovery of CH4 and NO2 during the waste treatment in the pig production activity, become a necessity to mitigate the negative environmental impacts. For the impact category TET, the main contribution was feed production (87%), being in contrast with previous LCA studies, like Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and Reckmann et al. (2012), who suggested that pig production is the main contributor. A very low contribution was observed for electricity (below 0.54%) as it was only 1.46 and 2.69 kWh for lighting and

Fig. 2. A breakdown of the impact categories: CC, FE, ME, TA and TET for A-1. CC: Climate Change as kg CO2-eq, FE: Freshwater eutrophication as kg P-eq, ME: Marine eutrophication as kg N-eq, TA: Terrestrial acidification as kg SO2-eq, TET: Terrestrial ecotoxicity as kg 1.4-Dichlorobenzene-eq.

pumping, respectively (Table 3). The contribution of the remaining activities was negligible. All impact categories were benefited from the use of residues (liquids and solids) as biofertilizer, avoiding the use of chemical fertilizers. A further discussion on the contribution of the activities to the impact categories is detailed below.

3.2. Breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities

In general, the breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities for the best PPT (A-1) was determined, considering as activities: pig production (including lagooning for manure treat- ment), feed production, electricity, water consumption, feed

transportation and avoided fertilizer production (Fig. 2). Pig pro- duction activity was identified as the main contributor to the global impact, with contributions from 75 to 89% for CC, FE, ME and TA, mainly because of CH4 and NO2 emissions during lagooning. Then, the recovery of CH4 and NO2 during the waste treatment in the pig production activity, become a necessity to mitigate the negative environmental impacts. For the impact category TET, the main contribution was feed production (87%), being in contrast with previous LCA studies, like Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and Reckmann et al. (2012), who suggested that pig production is the main contributor. A very low contribution was observed for electricity (below 0.54%) as it was only 1.46 and 2.69 kWh for lighting and

pumping, respectively (Table 3). The contribution of the remaining activities was negligible. All impact categories were benefited from the use of residues (liquids and solids) as biofertilizer, avoiding the use of chemical fertilizers. 3.2. Breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities

In general, the breakdown of the environmental impacts by activities for the best PPT (A-1) was determined, considering as activities: pig production (including lagooning for manure treat- ment), feed production, electricity, water consumption, feed

transportation and avoided fertilizer production (Fig. 2). Pig pro- duction activity was identified as the main contributor to the global impact, with contributions from 75 to 89% for CC, FE, ME and TA, mainly because of CH4 and NO2 emissions during lagooning. Then, the recovery of CH4 and NO2 during the waste treatment in the pig production activity, become a necessity to mitigate the negative environmental impacts. For the impact category TET, the main contribution was feed production (87%), being in contrast with previous LCA studies, like Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and Reckmann et al. (2012), who suggested that pig production is the main contributor. A very low contribution was observed for electricity (below 0.54%) as it was only 1.46 and 2.69 kWh for lighting and

pumping, respectively (Table 3). The contribution of the remaining activities was negligible. All impact categories were benefited from the use of residues (liquids and solids) as biofertilizer, avoiding the use of chemical fertilizers. 40: <header>3.3. Contribution of the activities to the impact categories </header>

Climate change (CC): The pig production activity contributed to more than 75% (5.75 kg CO2-eq) in this impact category. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of corn and soybeans) follows it (15.97%). The contribution of the remaining categories was discrete. The flows contributing the most were the GHG emissions of CH4, NO2 and CO2 (83.72%, 11.61 and 3.51%, respectively), which came from the enteric emissions and the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter in lagoons. These results are in accordance with the study reported by Stone et al. (2010), which suggested pig production as the activity with the highest contribution to CC (82.8%), especially because of enteric emission. However, Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported that feed production is the major contributor of CC, taking into account the pig supply chain (neglected in this study). Fertilizer substitutions make a beneficial contribution to CC.

Freshwater eutrophication (FE) and marine eutrophication (ME): The activity with the highest contribution to FE was also pig pro- duction, with values above 89%. CH4 (93.49%) and NO2 (4.38%) were the flows with the main contributions. Feed production contributed to 7.91% (cultivation and harvest of corn (4.07%) and soybeans (3.84%)), mainly due to the generation of phosphorus leachates during fertilizer application in agricultural activities, which is transported to water reservoirs, going through oligotrophic, mesotrophic and eutrophic stages and causing an excessive pri- mary production (Correll, 1998). In case of ME, pig production ac- tivity has also the highest contribution to marine eutrophication with 93.78%, followed by feed production (cultivation and harvest of corn (4.07%) and soybeans (3.84%). GHG emissions obtained from lagooning, (i.e., CH4 and NO2) affect the most, contributing to 93.79% and 4.38%, respectively.

The comparison with other pig production LCA studies is diffi- cult, due to the variety of system boundaries and functional units considered. Nevertheless, the values of the present study agree with the results reported by Gutiérrez et al., 2016, who suggested that pig production contributes the most to eutrophication (79%). These results are in contrast with previous studies, which referred

to cultivation and harvest as the activities with the highest envi- ronmental burdens (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015; Lamnatou et al., 2016), with eutro- phication values over 60%, due to environmental burdens associ- ated to agricultural activities in feed production.

Nutrient diffusion in aquatic bodies (N and P) is the main cause of FE and ME (Goedkoop et al., 2009). In addition, different studies suggested that GHG affecting CC (CO2, CH4 and NO2 mainly) (Eggleston et al., 2006) has a significant impact in aquatic bodies causing eutrophication (Fragoso et al., 2011). In case of pig pro- duction, most of the emissions come from the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter in lagoons and from enteric fermentations, increasing FE and ME, mainly as a consequence of the reduction of water transparency (Fragoso et al., 2011). The main factors are: 1) elevation of temperature that stimulates the estab- lishment of cyanobacteria, where the vertical control of the zooplankton is reduced (Gliwicz, 1990), bringing with it an important bacterial activity, enhancing the internal load of nutri- ents (Jensen and Andersen, 1982); and 2) the resuspension that occurs, multiplying the probability of the appearance of strong winds (Schelske et al., 1995) and causing an augmentation in the nutrient load due to the precipitation of rainfall in the winter season (Mooij et al., 2005).

Therefore, the emissions of GHG do not only affect CC, but also ME and FE. Previous studies on pig production in Cuba do not emphasize in the relationship between eutrophication and polluting gas emissions. The environmental impacts of the gaseous emissions influenced CC, ME and FE simultaneously suggesting that lagooning is the hotspot for process improvements. These is important for decision-makers as an instrument to minimize the pollution; reduce environmental problems, conflicts and risks (including civil and penal responsibility); and improve the com- pany image in market.

Terrestrial acidification (TA): Pig production and lagooning were the main contributors to this impact category with 0.06 kg SO2-eq (86.37%), followed by cultivation and harvest of soybeans with 0.58E-02 kg SO2-eq (8.37%). The activity pig production was again the highest contributors to total acidifying substances due to the effect of NO2 (65.62%) and CH4 (31.78%) emissions; where NO2 comes from lagooning (97.51% NO and incineration of pig disposal corpses (2.49% NO2); and CH4 comes from lagooning (60.46% of CH4) and enteric emissions (39.54% of CH4). This remarks the ne- cessity for CH4 and NO2 recovery during the pig production activity.

Terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET): This impact category refers to the potential impact on terrestrial ecosystems of toxic substances (Guinée, 2002) as heavy metals (zinc, lead, nickel, cadmium, cop- per) (Haye et al., 2007). TET has a different trend comparing to the others impact categories. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of feed) has the highest impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity (75.48% for corn and 11.12% for soybeans) as compared to the other activities reported, due to the emissions of compounds from chemical fer- tilizers and herbicides used in feed cultivation. Further studies should be done on the effect of using digestate for feed cultivation in PPT. The substances that present the main contributions were atrazine (49.93%), metolachlor (11.78%) (despite its harmful effects to the environment, the use of atrazine and metolachlor are extensively used for the control of weeds in the United States (Fernandez-Cornejo et al., 2014)) and phosphorous (11.53%) (included in the life cycle inventory when considered corn and soybean as animal feed), agreeing with Landis et al. (2007). Pig production impact was 12.80%, being below 1% for the remaining activities.

In general, pig production was the activity affecting the most the impact categories in the Cuban PPT, reaching the highest values in four of these five impact categories; whereas feed production (corn

and soybeans) reached the highest impacts for TET. Therefore, cleaner production alternatives should be focused on minimizing the emissions during pig production and feed production activities. A breakdown for these activities is further discussed.

The activity pig production was again the highest contributors to total acidifying substances due to the effect of NO2 (65.62%) and CH4 (31.78%) emissions; where NO2 comes from lagooning (97.51% NO and incineration of pig disposal corpses (2.49% NO2); and CH4 comes from lagooning (60.46% of CH4) and enteric emissions (39.54% of CH4). This remarks the ne- cessity for CH4 and NO2 recovery during the pig production activity.

Terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET): This impact category refers to the potential impact on terrestrial ecosystems of toxic substances (Guinée, 2002) as heavy metals (zinc, lead, nickel, cadmium, cop- per) (Haye et al., 2007). TET has a different trend comparing to the others impact categories. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of feed) has the highest impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity (75.48% for corn and 11.12% for soybeans) as compared to the other activities reported, due to the emissions of compounds from chemical fer- tilizers and herbicides used in feed cultivation. Further studies should be done on the effect of using digestate for feed cultivation in PPT. The substances that present the main contributions were atrazine (49.93%), metolachlor (11.78%) (despite its harmful effects to the environment, the use of atrazine and metolachlor are extensively used for the control of weeds in the United States (Fernandez-Cornejo et al., 2014)) and phosphorous (11.53%) (included in the life cycle inventory when considered corn and soybean as animal feed), agreeing with Landis et al. (2007). Pig production impact was 12.80%, being below 1% for the remaining activities.

In general, pig production was the activity affecting the most the impact categories in the Cuban PPT, reaching the highest values in four of these five impact categories; whereas feed production (corn

and soybeans) reached the highest impacts for TET. Therefore, cleaner production alternatives should be focused on minimizing the emissions during pig production and feed production activities. A breakdown for these activities is further discussed.
43: <header>3.3. Contribution of the activities to the impact categories </header>

Climate change (CC): The pig production activity contributed to more than 75% (5.75 kg CO2-eq) in this impact category. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of corn and soybeans) follows it (15.97%). The contribution of the remaining categories was discrete. The flows contributing the most were the GHG emissions of CH4, NO2 and CO2 (83.72%, 11.61 and 3.51%, respectively), which came from the enteric emissions and the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter in lagoons. These results are in accordance with the study reported by Stone et al. (2010), which suggested pig production as the activity with the highest contribution to CC (82.8%), especially because of enteric emission. However, Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported that feed production is the major contributor of CC, taking into account the pig supply chain (neglected in this study). Fertilizer substitutions make a beneficial contribution to CC.

Freshwater eutrophication (FE) and marine eutrophication (ME): The activity with the highest contribution to FE was also pig pro- duction, with values above 89%. CH4 (93.49%) and NO2 (4.38%) were the flows with the main contributions. Feed production contributed to 7.91% (cultivation and harvest of corn (4.07%) and soybeans (3.84%)), mainly due to the generation of phosphorus leachates during fertilizer application in agricultural activities, which is transported to water reservoirs, going through oligotrophic, mesotrophic and eutrophic stages and causing an excessive pri- mary production (Correll, 1998). In case of ME, pig production ac- tivity has also the highest contribution to marine eutrophication with 93.78%, followed by feed production (cultivation and harvest of corn (4.07%) and soybeans (3.84%). GHG emissions obtained from lagooning, (i.e., CH4 and NO2) affect the most, contributing to 93.79% and 4.38%, respectively.

The comparison with other pig production LCA studies is diffi- cult, due to the variety of system boundaries and functional units considered. Nevertheless, the values of the present study agree with the results reported by Gutiérrez et al., 2016, who suggested that pig production contributes the most to eutrophication (79%). These results are in contrast with previous studies, which referred

to cultivation and harvest as the activities with the highest envi- ronmental burdens (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015; Lamnatou et al., 2016), with eutro- phication values over 60%, due to environmental burdens associ- ated to agricultural activities in feed production.

Nutrient diffusion in aquatic bodies (N and P) is the main cause of FE and ME (Goedkoop et al., 2009). In addition, different studies suggested that GHG affecting CC (CO2, CH4 and NO2 mainly) (Eggleston et al., 2006) has a significant impact in aquatic bodies causing eutrophication (Fragoso et al., 2011). In case of pig pro- duction, most of the emissions come from the uncontrolled decomposition of the organic matter in lagoons and from enteric fermentations, increasing FE and ME, mainly as a consequence of the reduction of water transparency (Fragoso et al., 2011). The main factors are: 1) elevation of temperature that stimulates the estab- lishment of cyanobacteria, where the vertical control of the zooplankton is reduced (Gliwicz, 1990), bringing with it an important bacterial activity, enhancing the internal load of nutri- ents (Jensen and Andersen, 1982); and 2) the resuspension that occurs, multiplying the probability of the appearance of strong winds (Schelske et al., 1995) and causing an augmentation in the nutrient load due to the precipitation of rainfall in the winter season (Mooij et al., 2005).

Therefore, the emissions of GHG do not only affect CC, but also ME and FE. Previous studies on pig production in Cuba do not emphasize in the relationship between eutrophication and polluting gas emissions. The environmental impacts of the gaseous emissions influenced CC, ME and FE simultaneously suggesting that lagooning is the hotspot for process improvements. These is important for decision-makers as an instrument to minimize the pollution; reduce environmental problems, conflicts and risks (including civil and penal responsibility); and improve the com- pany image in market.

Terrestrial acidification (TA): Pig production and lagooning were the main contributors to this impact category with 0.06 kg SO2-eq (86.37%), followed by cultivation and harvest of soybeans with 0.58E-02 kg SO2-eq (8.37%). The activity pig production was again the highest contributors to total acidifying substances due to the effect of NO2 (65.62%) and CH4 (31.78%) emissions; where NO2 comes from lagooning (97.51% NO and incineration of pig disposal corpses (2.49% NO2); and CH4 comes from lagooning (60.46% of CH4) and enteric emissions (39.54% of CH4). This remarks the ne- cessity for CH4 and NO2 recovery during the pig production activity.

Terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET): This impact category refers to the potential impact on terrestrial ecosystems of toxic substances (Guinée, 2002) as heavy metals (zinc, lead, nickel, cadmium, cop- per) (Haye et al., 2007). TET has a different trend comparing to the others impact categories. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of feed) has the highest impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity (75.48% for corn and 11.12% for soybeans) as compared to the other activities reported, due to the emissions of compounds from chemical fer- tilizers and herbicides used in feed cultivation. Further studies should be done on the effect of using digestate for feed cultivation in PPT. The substances that present the main contributions were atrazine (49.93%), metolachlor (11.78%) (despite its harmful effects to the environment, the use of atrazine and metolachlor are extensively used for the control of weeds in the United States (Fernandez-Cornejo et al., 2014)) and phosphorous (11.53%) (included in the life cycle inventory when considered corn and soybean as animal feed), agreeing with Landis et al. (2007). Pig production impact was 12.80%, being below 1% for the remaining activities.

In general, pig production was the activity affecting the most the impact categories in the Cuban PPT, reaching the highest values in four of these five impact categories; whereas feed production (corn

and soybeans) reached the highest impacts for TET. Therefore, cleaner production alternatives should be focused on minimizing the emissions during pig production and feed production activities. A breakdown for these activities is further discussed.

TET has a different trend comparing to the others impact categories. Feed production (cultivation and harvest of feed) has the highest impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity (75.48% for corn and 11.12% for soybeans) as compared to the other activities reported, due to the emissions of compounds from chemical fer- tilizers and herbicides used in feed cultivation. Further studies should be done on the effect of using digestate for feed cultivation in PPT. The substances that present the main contributions were atrazine (49.93%), metolachlor (11.78%) (despite its harmful effects to the environment, the use of atrazine and metolachlor are extensively used for the control of weeds in the United States (Fernandez-Cornejo et al., 2014)) and phosphorous (11.53%) (included in the life cycle inventory when considered corn and soybean as animal feed), agreeing with Landis et al. (2007). Pig production impact was 12.80%, being below 1% for the remaining activities.

In general, pig production was the activity affecting the most the impact categories in the Cuban PPT, reaching the highest values in four of these five impact categories; whereas feed production (corn

and soybeans) reached the highest impacts for TET. Therefore, cleaner production alternatives should be focused on minimizing the emissions during pig production and feed production activities. A breakdown for these activities is further discussed.
48: <header>3.4. Breakdown for pig productions and feed production activities </header>

Fig. 3 showed the environmental burdens associated to pig production (divided as pig housing and lagooning), as well as feed production with the two fundamental ingredients of the pig diet (corn and soybeans). For the studied impact categories, the highest contribution corresponded to lagooning (up to 86%), because of the emissions of CH4, NO2, CO2, COD, BOD5 and heavy metals. Emissions from lagooning have been reported as an important consequence of CC (Wiedemann et al., 2010). The values obtained for FE and ME were in agreement with Gutiérrez et al. (2016), reaching a contri- bution of around 57% for eutrophication. Acidification values of around 67% (as a partial contribution with other activities) have been also reported for pig production, in contrast with the present study (14%), because of the highest amounts of CH4 and NH3 ob- tained. In the case of TET, lagooning emissions are responsible for the highest impact (54%), especially because of heavy metals disposal to the ground.

The contribution of the feed components is also shown in Fig. 3. Despite the amount of corn in pig diet (48%) is lower than that of soybean, corn cultivation and harvest has the highest impact on the studied categories (always above 51%), agreeing with Eriksson et al. (2005) and Van der Werf et al. (2005). TET was the impact category affected the most, with values above 87%, which was related mainly to the agricultural and industrial process of corn and its greater intensity of fertilization compared to soybeans (use of herbicides, pesticides, insecticides and chemical fertilizers in agricultural stages) (Blonk Agri Footprint BV, 2015), emitting larger quantities of metal to soil, generating damages to ecosystems, and increasing the environmental burdens. As corn and soybean are largely imported from foreign countries, it is difficult to assess cleaner production scenarios. Initiatives for self-production of feed in Cuba can reduce significantly these impacts by recycling biofertilizers obtained from the anaerobic treatment of pig manure.

These results advised that cleaner production scenarios for the Cuban PPT should be focused on lagooning mainly, considering: 1) an appropriated COD and BOD5 removal efficiencies during lagooning; 2) the recovery of GHG emissions; and 3) the combi- nation of both: recovery of GHG emission by using high efficiency COD and BOD5 removal technologies.

Fig. 3. Environmental charges contributions of pig production and feed components in A-1. CC: Climate Change as kg CO2-eq, FE: Freshwater eutrophication as kg P-eq, ME: Marine eutrophication as kg N-eq, TA: Terrestrial acidification as kg SO2-eq, TET: Terrestrial ecotoxicity as kg 1.4-Dichlorobenzene-eq.

CC: Climate Change as kg CO2-eq, FE: Freshwater eutrophication as kg P-eq, ME: Marine eutrophication as kg N-eq, TA: Terrestrial acidification as kg SO2-eq, TET: Terrestrial ecotoxicity as kg 1.4-Dichlorobenzene-eq.
51: Three cleaner production scenarios (A-1.1, A-1.2 and A-1.3) were considered for reducing the environmental impacts during pig production activity, especially during lagooning, as it had the greatest contribution to the studied impact categories.

The first cleaner production scenario considered the effect of technical conditions during lagooning (Scenario A-1.1). Lagooning is widely used in Cuba for waste management, (Blanco et al., 2015; Gutiérrez et al., 2016). A conventional lagooning system could reach values of 64.2 and 67.2% of COD and BOD5, respectively (Sánchez- Morales et al., 2011). Although this is a low efficiency technology for the removal of organic matter, shortcomings associated with maintenance (e.g., vegetable presence in slopes, low cleaning, organic matter overload and accumulation of sludge at the bottom of the lagoon), may reduce COD and BOD5 removal efficiency (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006). For that reason, it was considered a removal efficiency of 30% for lagoons with a poor maintenance.

The second cleaner production scenario (Scenario A-1.2) assessed the recovery of GHG in the small scale digesters installed in most of the Cuban piggeries (i.e. Fixed Dome Digesters, FDD) with a low organic matter removal efficiency (50%). In Cuba, the implementation of piggery waste treatment systems is mandatory (Gaceta Oficial, 1997; NC-27, 1999), to recover and reduce CH4 emissions. AD technologies are typically used to that aim (Chynoweth et al., 2001). FDD are the most widely used technology in Cuba with around 1950 plants (Ministerio de Comercio Exterior la Inversión Extranjera, 2017) for the treatment of pig manure. This technology is known for its low organic matter removal efficiency from 40 to 50% (considered as 50% in this study) (Guardia-Puebla et al., 2017; Nzila et al., 2012) due to its cylindrical shape that does not guarantee the uniform path of the substrate inside the digester, causing hydraulic short circuits (Montalvo and Guerrero, 2003). The biogas produced is used for the generation of heat, replacing conventional fuel consumption; the solid effluent (digestate) is employed as organic fertilizer to replace fertilizers rich in N, P and K. Furthermore, the wastewater can be poured into a lagooning system to reduce its organic load for final disposal (Pérez et al., 2016). The biogas generated was considered as avoided product (fuel for heat generation).

The third cleaner production scenario (Scenario A-1.3) consid- ered the recovery of GHG emission in high efficiency AD technol- logies (i.e. Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Bed, UASB) with a high COD removal efficiency (80-90%). UASB reactors are High rate anaerobic digesters widely used over the world (Frankin, 2001). They possess a high removal efficiency of organic matter, producing a high amount of biogas per unit of reactor volume in comparison to FDD (Moletta, 2005). COD removal efficiencies of 80% have been re- ported (Kaparaju et al., 2010; Moletta, 2005; Rodrigues et al., 2010).

A comparison of the environmental impacts for the cleaner production scenarios A-1.1, A-1.2 and A-1.3 with respect to the base scenario A-1, for the impact categories CC, FE, ME, TA and TET, is shown in Fig. 4.

As a consequence of low maintenance in lagoons, low organic matter removal and high odors emission occur (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006). When reducing waste treatment efficiency in A- 1.1 with respect to A-1, the impact categories increased by 0.88 kg CO2-eq (CC), 2.55 kg P-eq (FE), 2.55 kg N-eq (ME) and 0.06 kg SO2-eq (TA). In the case of TET, a similar distribution was observed (harvest and cultivation of feed remain the same). In addition, CH4 emitted from lagooning in A-1.1, caused 85% of the impact on CC (6.63kg CO2-eq) and 94% over FE and ME (19.10 kg N and P-eq, respectively). The emissions of NO2 contributed to 10.28% (0.80 kg CO2-eq) in CC and to 3.83% in FE and in ME (0.77 kg N and 0.77 P-eq, respectively). In the case of TA, NH3 contributes to 53% (0.08 kg SO2-

0: <title>Y.A. Reyes et al. / Journal of Cleaner Production 219 (2019) 99-109 </title>

Fig. 4. Comparison of the three cleaner production alternatives profiles characterized (A-1.1, A-1.2 and A-1.3) with respect to the base scenario (A-1). CC: Climate Change as kg CO2-eq, FE: Freshwater eutrophication as kg P-eq, ME: Marine eutrophication as kg N-eq, TA: Terrestrial acidification as 10-2 kg SO2-eq, TET: Terrestrial ecotoxicity as 10- kg 1.4- Dichlorobenzene-eq.
eq) and NO2 to the 35.23% (0.05 kg SO2-eq).

In general, it was showed that low maintenance in lagooning increases the environmental impacts on the studied categories. For that reason, a proper operation of lagoons is mandatory, following maintenance procedures, such as cleaning of the pretreatment units (if any), installation of flow meters, perimeter fences, roads, gardening, and the removal of accumulated sludge; and when operational problems are detected, it is advisable to adopt correc- tive actions (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006). Additionally, lagoons in Cuba are not provided with impermeable blanket to cover the bottom, which cause leachates of polluting substances to under- ground waters. This effect was not quantified in this work, being a research necessity for further studies.

When comparing A-1.2 with the base scenario A-1, the former reduces the environmental impact (above 13%) due to the recovery of GHG. The CC impact category decreased by 13% (0.81 kg CO2-eq) because of a decrease of the emissions into the air, while FE and ME were reduced by 15% (2.34 kg N-eq and 2.34 kg P-eq, respectively). TA and TET remain the same. This suggested that the imple- mentation of FDD has a beneficial environmental impact by avoiding GHG emissions recovering CH4 for fuel substitution and by considering digestate as nutrient-rich fertilizer.

The effect of using high efficiency AD technologies (i.e. UASB) over small scale digesters (i.e. FDD) could be assessed by comparing A-1.3 with A-1.2. As it was expected, a decrease of environmental impacts was observed for CC, FE and ME (2.70 kg CO2-eq, 55%; 7.78 kg P-eq and 7.78 kg N-eq 49%, respectively) when the effi- ciency of the AD technology increased. No changes were observed for TA due to the fact that the emissions mainly of NO2 and NH3 were constant in the inventory; as well as for TET, because metals emissions remain constant, keeping stable their bioavailability. The increase of the COD removal efficiency increased the biogas pro- duction, increasing the fuel substitution and reducing GHG emis- sions in A-1.3 with respect to A-1.2.

It should be noted, that the highest benefits with respect to the base scenario (A-1) was obtained when high efficiency AD tech- nologies (A-1.3) were considered. In that case, a reduction of CC by 49% was observed (3.35 kg CO2-eq), together with a decrease of FE and ME by 43% (7.53 kg P-eq and kg N-eq).

In general, the highest environmental benefits for the PPT can be obtained by using a high efficiency AD technology, while low im- provements can be obtained when using FDD. The technical con- ditions of the lagoons could also affect the environmental burdens of the PPT and a proper maintenance become crucial.

The increase of the COD removal efficiency increased the biogas pro- duction, increasing the fuel substitution and reducing GHG emis- sions in A-1.3 with respect to A-1.2.

It should be noted, that the highest benefits with respect to the base scenario (A-1) was obtained when high efficiency AD tech- nologies (A-1.3) were considered. In that case, a reduction of CC by 49% was observed (3.35 kg CO2-eq), together with a decrease of FE and ME by 43% (7.53 kg P-eq and kg N-eq).

In general, the highest environmental benefits for the PPT can be obtained by using a high efficiency AD technology, while low im- provements can be obtained when using FDD. The technical con- ditions of the lagoons could also affect the environmental burdens of the PPT and a proper maintenance become crucial.

eq) and NO2 to the 35.23% (0.05 kg SO2-eq).

In general, it was showed that low maintenance in lagooning increases the environmental impacts on the studied categories. For that reason, a proper operation of lagoons is mandatory, following maintenance procedures, such as cleaning of the pretreatment units (if any), installation of flow meters, perimeter fences, roads, gardening, and the removal of accumulated sludge; and when operational problems are detected, it is advisable to adopt correc- tive actions (Gutiérrez and Marrero, 2006). Additionally, lagoons in Cuba are not provided with impermeable blanket to cover the bottom, which cause leachates of polluting substances to under- ground waters. This effect was not quantified in this work, being a research necessity for further studies.

When comparing A-1.2 with the base scenario A-1, the former reduces the environmental impact (above 13%) due to the recovery of GHG. The CC impact category decreased by 13% (0.81 kg CO2-eq) because of a decrease of the emissions into the air, while FE and ME were reduced by 15% (2.34 kg N-eq and 2.34 kg P-eq, respectively). TA and TET remain the same. This suggested that the imple- mentation of FDD has a beneficial environmental impact by avoiding GHG emissions recovering CH4 for fuel substitution and by considering digestate as nutrient-rich fertilizer.

The increase of the COD removal efficiency increased the biogas pro- duction, increasing the fuel substitution and reducing GHG emis- sions in A-1.3 with respect to A-1.2.

It should be noted, that the highest benefits with respect to the base scenario (A-1) was obtained when high efficiency AD tech- nologies (A-1.3) were considered. In that case, a reduction of CC by 49% was observed (3.35 kg CO2-eq), together with a decrease of FE and ME by 43% (7.53 kg P-eq and kg N-eq).

In general, the highest environmental benefits for the PPT can be obtained by using a high efficiency AD technology, while low im- provements can be obtained when using FDD. The technical con- ditions of the lagoons could also affect the environmental burdens of the PPT and a proper maintenance become crucial.

5: The Life Cycle Environmental Impacts of the three Pig Produc- tion Technologies (Genetic Farms, Multiplier Farms and Production Farms) showed that Genetic Farms produce the lowest impacts on climate change (CC), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), terrestrial acidification (TA) and terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET). These results were attributed to the lower con- sumption of feed (soybean and corn) and energy, and the lower global emissions of pollutants in the Genetic Farms. Pig production was the activity with the highest contribution (75-89%) to the impact categories CC, TA, ME and FE, due to the emissions of greenhouse gases during lagooning. In contrast, feed production (corn and soybeans) reached the highest impacts (87%) for TET, which was related to the agricultural and industrial processes of corn and soybean.

It was also shown that cleaner production opportunities can mitigate the environmental impact in Pig Farms. Especially the use of high efficiency anaerobic digestion technologies (i.e. Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket Reactors) over Fixed Dome Digester and lagooning will produce the highest benefits by reducing up to 57% the impacts on CC, FE and ME. In addition, the technical conditions of the lagoons can also affect also the environmental profile of Pig Production Technologies when low maintenance occurs, increasing by 0.88 CO2-eq, 2.55 kg P-eq, 2.55 kg N-eq and 0.06 kg SO2-eq, the impact on the categories CC, FE, ME and TA, respectively.
